---
title: "Extras & Dependency Groups"
tagline: "Or what to Do When Some Dependencies Are Optional"
type: scenario
tool: python
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When Some Dependencies Are Optional*
Python merges two JVM ideas into one scenario. **Extras** are optional *runtime* features a package offers (`demo-lib-a[feature]`) — the sibling of Maven optional dependencies ([MS8](../../maven-demo/scenario-8-optional/guide.qmd)). **Dependency groups** are optional *development* sets (test, docs, lint) — the sibling of build scopes ([MS7](../../maven-demo/scenario-7-scopes/guide.qmd)). This scenario shows both.

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
Tested with **pip 25.3**, **Poetry 2.4**, **uv 0.11**. Extras are standard (PEP 508) everywhere. Dependency groups differ: Poetry groups arrived in 1.2; uv uses `[dependency-groups]` (PEP 735); pip has no group concept and uses multiple requirements files instead.
:::

#### ! Example

Extras — opting in pulls the optional `demo-lib-d`:

```{.text include="pip/requirements-extras.txt" snippet="p7-extras"}
```

Groups — a dev-only set, installed or skipped on demand:

```{.toml include="uv/pyproject.toml" snippet="p7-uv-group"}
```

#### ! Expect
Optional dependencies come along by default.

#### ! Actual
They don't — that's the point of "optional". Plain `demo-lib-a` does **not** install `demo-lib-d`; `demo-lib-a[feature]` does. Likewise the `dev` group is absent from a default install and present only when you ask (`--with dev` in Poetry, the default-but-skippable `--no-dev` in uv). pip has no group mechanism at all: you keep `requirements.txt` and `requirements-dev.txt` as separate files.

#### ! Why
Extras and groups both answer "don't make everyone pay for what only some need", but at different layers. An extra is published *by the library* as a named bundle of optional runtime deps — consumers opt in with `[name]`. A group is defined *by your project* for its own workflow (tests, docs) and never ships to consumers. The JVM splits these across `<optional>true</optional>` and build scopes; Python's extras + groups cover the same ground with different seams.

#### ! Fix
*   **Offering optional features in a library:** declare an extra, so consumers write `your-lib[feature]`.
*   **Separating dev tooling:** use dependency groups (Poetry `group`, uv `[dependency-groups]`); on pip, a second requirements file.
*   **Installing:** `pip install "pkg[extra]"`; `poetry install --with dev`; `uv sync` (groups) — check what your default install actually pulls.

#### ! Risk
* LOW Declaring a clean extra for a genuinely optional feature.
* MEDIUM Putting test/lint tools in main dependencies instead of a group — every consumer and every production image now carries pytest.
* HIGH Relying on an extra being installed when your deploy step omits it; the feature imports fine locally and `ImportError`s in production.

#### ! Check

**Fast check:**

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt        && ! pip show demo-lib-d   # absent
pip install -r requirements-extras.txt &&   pip show demo-lib-d   # present
poetry install --without dev  # no demo-lib-d
poetry install --with dev     # demo-lib-d present
uv sync --no-dev              # no demo-lib-d
uv sync                       # demo-lib-d present
```

**What to look for:** `demo-lib-d` present only when the extra or group is requested.

**Conclusion:** Optional means opt-in — extras for runtime features, groups for dev tooling.

#### ! Takeaway
Python folds the JVM's optional dependencies and build scopes into extras (runtime, published by the library) and dependency groups (dev, defined by your project). pip has extras but no groups — mind the gap if you switch tools.

#### ! Related
*   [P4](../scenario-4-specifiers/guide.qmd) - Version specifiers, which apply inside extras and groups too.
*   [MS7](../../maven-demo/scenario-7-scopes/guide.qmd) - JVM build scopes (the group analogue).
*   [MS8](../../maven-demo/scenario-8-optional/guide.qmd) - JVM optional dependencies (the extra analogue).

#### ! Wild
The classic extras example is `pip install "requests[security]"` — for years that pulled cryptography-backed TLS extras that plain `requests` left out. It was also a classic footgun: countless bug reports of "requests can't do X" turned out to be "you installed requests, not requests[the-extra-you-needed]".
